content.description:
**Status:** In progress

## Summary

A separate `structured-note` type for exploring enriched notes with reactive computed values and transclusions. Uses [Reactive Computed Fields](item://c0c0c0c0-0074-0000-0000-000000000000) as the single underlying mechanism — a transclusion is just a computed field whose formula mirrors another item's field. Existing notes remain untouched; this is a parallel experiment that can be abandoned at any point.

## The Unification

The original version of this proposal introduced four Hob page primitives (`text`, `transclude`, `val`, `link`) and a new page format where pages are Hob expressions. Through iteration, a simpler insight emerged: all of these collapse into one mechanism — computed fields with `get-field` as uniform access.

| What you want | What it actually is |
|---|---|
| Transclusion of another note | `(get-field @other-note :description)` — a computed field mirroring another item's field |
| Inline computed value | `(* (get-field :self :quantity) (get-field :self :price))` — a computed field with logic |
| Functional transclusion (`?call=`) | `(some-function @target-item)` — a computed field whose formula is a function call |
| Aggregation | `(reduce + 0 (map (fn [i] (get-field i :amount)) (query-tagged @expense-tag)))` — a computed field over a query |

One mechanism. One reactive system. One way to track dependencies. No special transclusion primitives needed.

## The Structured Note Type

A new item type, separate from the existing note type.

```json
{
  "type": "<structured-note-type-id>",
  "content": {
    "body": "Here is a summary: {{summary}}. The total budget is **{{total-budget}}**.",
    "quantity": 10,
    "unit-price": 25.50,
    "computed": {
      "summary": ["get-field", "@other-note", ":description"],
      "total-budget": ["*", ["get-field", ":self", ":quantity"],
                            ["get-field", ":self", ":unit-price"]]
    }
  }
}
```

- `content.body` — markdown string with `{{field-name}}` references. Prose formatting (bold, italic, headings, lists) handled by markdown-it.
- `content.computed` — map of field names to Hob formulas. Evaluated lazily via `get-field`. See [Reactive Computed Fields](item://c0c0c0c0-0074-0000-0000-000000000000).
- Static fields (`quantity`, `unit-price`) coexist alongside computed fields normally.

## Authoring Experience

The editor preserves the feel of writing a note:

- **Prose**: type normally in CodeMirror — markdown, same as today.
- **Field references**: insert `{{field-name}}` anywhere in the body to reference a named field (static or computed).
- **Links**: use existing `item://id` syntax — unchanged.
- **Field management**: a side panel or inspector for creating and editing computed fields — name, Hob formula, current value.

One syntax, one concept: `{{field-name}}` always means "resolve this field via `get-field`." Whether the field is a transclusion, a calculation, or a static value is defined in the field itself, not in the body syntax.

### Editor gestures

Inserting a transclusion is two logical steps — create the computed field, then reference it — but the editor streamlines this into a single gesture: an "insert transclusion" action prompts for a target item and a field name, then creates both the `content.computed` entry and the `{{name}}` reference at the cursor.

### Storage

What you see in the editor is what's stored. `{{field-name}}` in the body maps 1:1 to a key in `content.computed` (or a static field in `content`). No compilation step, no generated names, trivial round-tripping.

## Rendering

The view evaluates the body:

1. Parse `content.body` with markdown-it (handles prose formatting).
2. For each `{{field-name}}`, call `(get-field :self :field-name)`.
3. `get-field` evaluates the formula (if computed) or returns the static value.
4. Reactive dependencies are tracked — if a referenced item or field changes, the note re-renders.
5. Transclusion results are rendered inline as formatted content.

The reactive infrastructure already exists — views use the same dependency tracking. No new rendering system needed.

## Implementation Progress

### Completed

- **Type definition** (`e8d7c6b5-...-5c4b`): `structured-note` type extending ATOM, with `preferredView` pointing to the readonly view.
- **`get-field` function** in the Hob interpreter: unified field access with computed field support. Resolves item refs, keywords, and string field names. Checks `content.computed` for formulas, evaluates via Hob evaluator, falls back to item root then `content`. Includes cycle detection via `_computingFields` Set.
- **`str/index-of`** added to Hob stdlib (needed by `resolve-body`).
- **Readonly view** (`e8d7c6b5-...-5c4c`): Hob view that resolves `{{field-name}}` references in the body via `get-field`, wraps resolved values in `<span class=\"field-ref\">` with dashed underline styling, renders markdown, and shows a collapsible \"Computed Fields\" summary listing field names and current values. Field ref spans are clickable — they scroll to the corresponding entry in the summary with a highlight animation.
- **Editable view** (`e8d7c6b5-...-5c4d`): JS view delegating to `generic-view` with `ui_hints` for name (heading), tags, body (markdown-editable), and computed fields.
- **`field-view-computed-fields`** (`e8d7c6b5-...-5c4e`): Custom field view rendering `content.computed` as name/formula pairs. Each formula is edited via `field-view-hob-structural`. Supports add/remove/rename of fields.

### Known Limitations

- **`:self` resolution in Hob views**: `get-field :self` doesn't resolve during Hob view rendering. Workaround: pass `(:id item)` directly instead of `:self`.
- **Cross-item reactivity**: The view only re-renders when its own item changes. If a computed field references another item and that item changes, the view doesn't re-render. This is an item-level tracking limitation.
- **No caching**: Computed fields are re-evaluated on every access. Fine for the experiment; caching is a future optimization.
- **No error sentinels**: Formula errors are swallowed (value shows as empty). The proposal describes error items and `ERR` links — not yet implemented.

### Next Steps

- Resolve the provenance/linking question (see below).
- Test arithmetic formulas and chained transclusions.
- Author real notes to evaluate the experience.
- Consider cross-item reactivity improvements if the experiment proves valuable.

## Experiment Plan

1. ~~Create the `structured-note` type definition.~~ Done.
2. ~~Implement the [Reactive Computed Fields](item://c0c0c0c0-0074-0000-0000-000000000000) mechanism.~~ Done (basic `get-field` with cycle detection; caching deferred).
3. ~~Build a basic view and editor for structured notes.~~ Done.
4. Author real notes using the new type.
5. Evaluate: does this add meaningful value over plain markdown notes?
6. If yes — refine the editor, build migration tooling for existing notes.
7. If no — delete the type and view items, lose nothing.

## Open Design Question: Provenance and Linking

The existing transclusion system adds chrome around transcluded content — a visual indicator and a link to the source item (including scroll-to-symbol for `?call=` functional transclusions). Structured notes need equivalent provenance, but the computed fields model makes this harder: a `{{field-name}}` reference doesn't directly encode its source — the formula in `content.computed` does.

The current readonly view provides:
- **Visual markers**: resolved values are wrapped in `<span class=\"field-ref\">` with a dashed underline, so the user can distinguish computed values from static text.
- **Field summary**: a collapsible section listing all computed fields with their current values.
- **Click-to-scroll**: clicking a field ref in the body scrolls to its entry in the summary.

What's missing: **links to source items and functions**. The three kinds of computed field reference each need different provenance:

| Formula pattern | What to link to | Example |
|---|---|---|
| `(get-field @item :field)` | The referenced item | Navigate to item, optionally to the specific field |
| `(some-function @item)` | Both the function definition and the target item | Navigate to the code item at the function symbol, and/or to the target item |
| `(* (get-field :self :x) ...)` | Nothing external — self-referential | Show the formula in the summary (already done) |
| Complex formula with multiple refs | All referenced items and functions | Multiple links |

### Approaches Under Consideration

**A. AST inspection + symbol search.** Walk each formula's JSON AST to find `@`-prefixed item references (cheap data lookup). For function names, search code items' `_symbols` metadata to find the defining item. Two separate mechanisms, but both are data lookups with no evaluation needed. Gets working links sooner with less risk.

**B. Runtime dependency tracking.** Have `get-field` and the evaluator record which items and functions were accessed during formula evaluation. Single mechanism that handles all cases (including nested references and dynamic dispatch), but requires plumbing state through the evaluation context.

**C. Hybrid.** Use AST inspection for the common cases (`@`-refs are trivial to find), and runtime tracking only for complex scenarios where static analysis isn't sufficient.

No decision made yet. The choice depends on how complex real-world formulas become and whether the simpler approach (A) covers enough cases to be useful.

## What This Enables

- **Reactive transclusions** that update when the source changes — same `get-field` mechanism as any computed field.
- **Live computed values** inline in prose — item counts, field references, aggregations, projections.
- **Functional transclusions** — applying a function to an item and embedding the result, replacing the `?call=` URL parameter convention with a proper computed field.
- **Queryable references** — computed field formulas are JSON AST, walkable and inspectable without parsing markdown.
- **Spreadsheet-like calculations** across items — budgets, projections, derived metrics.
- **One mechanism** for all reactive data — no separate systems for transclusions, computed values, and aggregations.

## What This Does Not Do

- **Replace notes.** This is a separate type. Existing notes and their markdown editing experience are unchanged.
- **Replace markdown for prose.** Bold, italic, headings, lists — still markdown, still rendered by markdown-it.
- **Require learning Hob to write notes.** Transclusions are created via an editor gesture — pick an item, name the field, done. Writing Hob formulas is opt-in for computed values.
- **Change the item model.** Items still have `content` maps. `content.computed` is a new convention, not a schema change.

## References

- [Reactive Computed Fields](item://c0c0c0c0-0074-0000-0000-000000000000) — The underlying mechanism: computed fields, `get-field`, caching, reactivity, cycle detection.
- [Hob Language Design Direction](item://c0c0c0c0-0058-0000-0000-000000000000) — Hob as the primary user-programming language.
- [Rich Transclusion Support](item://c0c0c0c0-0035-0000-0000-000000000000) — The existing transclusion system in markdown notes.
